items.headline: Legora hits $5.6B valuation as legal AI rivalry intensifies with Harvey
items.body:
Legora, a Swedish legal AI startup, has raised $50 million in a Series D extension that values the company at $5.6 billion. The round, which included Nvidia's venture fund NVentures alongside Atlassian and other investors, comes just one month after the startup closed a $550 million Series D. The timing underscores how quickly the legal AI market is consolidating around two dominant players—Legora and U.S. competitor Harvey—each backed by substantial capital and high-profile enterprise customers.

## Scaling to $100M ARR

Legora reached $100 million in annual recurring revenue between its two Series D rounds, crossing a critical profitability threshold that justified a near-$6 billion valuation. The company, which launched its platform only 18 months ago, now operates across 50 markets and serves more than 1,000 law firms and in-house legal teams. Major clients include top-tier firms like Bird & Bird, Cleary Gottlieb, and Linklaters. Harvey, by contrast, claims 100,000 lawyers across 1,300 organizations—a significantly larger installed base that includes firms like Latham & Watkins and corporate legal teams at companies including T-Mobile and Bridgewater. Harvey's $11 billion valuation, set last month when Sequoia tripled down on its investment, reflects that customer density advantage.

## The Moat Question and Foundation Model Risk

Both companies are pursuing geographic expansion as a competitive battleground: Legora is prioritizing U.S. penetration while Harvey pushes into Europe. They have also escalated into consumer marketing, with Harvey signing an endorsement deal featuring actor Gabriel Macht and Legora launching a campaign with Jude Law. Yet both face a structural vulnerability. Their platforms run on large language models built by AI infrastructure giants—Anthropic, OpenAI—who could theoretically become competitors. When Anthropic recently launched a legal plugin for Claude, public legal software companies saw stock declines. Legora's CEO Max Junestrand argues that "the real value is in how they're applied," not the underlying models. Nvidia's willingness to invest signals confidence that Legora has built defensible application-layer moats, though Nvidia's simultaneous backing of both Anthropic and OpenAI suggests it is hedging against any single player dominating the market. For enterprises evaluating legal AI vendors, the question is whether either startup can maintain pricing power and customer lock-in if foundation models commoditize legal use cases.
items.original_title: Legal AI startup Legora hits $5.6B valuation and its battle with Harvey just got hotter

items.headline: Researchers engineer bacteria to survive with 19 amino acids instead of 20
items.body:
Researchers at Columbia and Harvard have successfully redesigned portions of *E. coli* to function without isoleucine, one of the 20 amino acids that comprise the universal genetic code. The work tests a longstanding hypothesis: that early life used fewer amino acids and simpler genetic codes before evolving into modern organisms. By demonstrating that cells can tolerate the loss of an amino acid through systematic protein redesign, the team has validated both the feasibility of the hypothesis and the maturity of AI-driven protein engineering tools.

## Swapping One Amino Acid Across the Ribosome

The researchers selected isoleucine as their target because it is chemically similar to leucine and valine—all three are hydrophobic amino acids with branched carbon-hydrogen structures. Analysis of the *E. coli* genome showed that isoleucine was the most frequently substituted amino acid across related species, suggesting it might be dispensable. Starting with 36 essential genes, the team replaced every isoleucine with valine. Fourteen of those replacements proved tolerable; 22 killed the cells, though one gene accommodated 45 individual substitutions without apparent harm.

To test isoleucine removal more rigorously, the researchers focused on the ribosome—the cellular machinery that translates genetic information into proteins. Using deep-learning protein-design software, they redesigned 25 of 32 ribosomal proteins that could not tolerate simple amino acid swaps. For the remaining five problem proteins, they allowed the software to alter amino acids structurally adjacent to isoleucine positions, compensating for potential disruptions to protein folding. This approach succeeded for four of five. The team then began replacing genes along a 10,000-base genomic segment encoding the ribosome's small subunit. Replacing 17 of 21 genes slowed cell growth; replacing 18 killed the cells entirely.

## What This Reveals About Ancient Life and Modern Tools

The work is not aimed at creating a superior organism but at understanding the evolutionary space of possible genetic codes. The consistent finding—that cells tolerate isoleucine loss with reduced fitness rather than outright failure—supports the theory that early life experimented with partial genetic codes. The successful use of AI-driven protein redesign suggests these tools have matured enough to handle the inverse problem: not adding new amino acids for novel chemistry, but removing them to explore what chemistry remains possible.

For practitioners, this demonstrates that protein-design software can now tackle problems requiring coordinated changes across multiple proteins and structures. The iterative refinement using multiple software packages, combined with structural reasoning about compensatory mutations, provides a template for complex multi-protein optimization tasks beyond this specific application.
items.original_title: Researchers try to cut the genetic code from 20 to 19 amino acids

items.headline: OpenAI restricts cybersecurity AI after Altman criticized Anthropic's same tactic
items.body:
OpenAI is rolling out GPT-5.5 Cyber, a tool designed to identify and exploit security vulnerabilities, exclusively to vetted cybersecurity professionals—a move that directly contradicts Sam Altman's recent criticism of Anthropic for doing the same with its Mythos cybersecurity tool. The decision underscores mounting tension between competing approaches to releasing powerful dual-use AI systems and raises questions about whether access restrictions are principled security measures or marketing theater.

## The Access Model

OpenAI confirmed on Thursday that Cyber will roll out to "critical cyber defenders" over the coming days through an application process requiring users to verify their credentials and explain their intended use. The tool performs penetration testing, vulnerability identification and exploitation, and malware reverse engineering—capabilities explicitly designed to help organizations harden defenses but equally capable of enabling attacks.

Weeks earlier, Altman publicly criticized Anthropic's restricted release of Mythos, calling the gatekeeping strategy "fear-based marketing" and suggesting the rhetoric was overblown. Other observers concurred that Anthropic's framing appeared exaggerated. Yet an unauthorized group reportedly accessed Mythos anyway despite the restrictions, undermining the premise that limiting distribution would prevent misuse.

## Dual-Use Dilemma Without Resolution

The parallel rollouts expose how AI companies are adopting comparable risk-mitigation strategies even while publicly disparaging them. OpenAI says it is consulting with the U.S. government and working to identify legitimate cybersecurity practitioners to expand Cyber access more broadly, positioning this as a methodical path toward wider availability. Anthropic followed a similar trajectory with Mythos.

For enterprise security teams, the pattern matters. Both tools represent genuinely useful capabilities for defense, but access bottlenecks delay organizational adoption and may fragment the market. If both companies ultimately expand access substantially—as their rhetoric suggests they intend—the current restrictions function as temporary gatekeeping rather than permanent barriers. The question for practitioners is whether these early-access periods yield meaningful intelligence that informs broader policy, or whether they primarily serve to soften public perception. Given that Mythos was accessed without authorization despite controls, security leaders should assume powerful dual-use capabilities will circulate regardless of official restrictions and plan accordingly.
items.original_title: After dissing Anthropic for limiting Mythos, OpenAI restricts access to Cyber, too

items.headline: Musk Sues OpenAI Over Nonprofit Pivot; Meta Layoffs Signal AI Job Disruption Limits
items.body:
Elon Musk's lawsuit against OpenAI and executives Sam Altman and Greg Brockman opened in federal court this week in Oakland, California, centering on whether the company abandoned its founding mission and misled Musk into contributing millions. The case hinges on OpenAI's structural peculiarity: a nonprofit parent company that created a for-profit subsidiary to raise capital, now attempting to convert into a public benefit corporation. The stakes extend beyond billionaire rivalry. A ruling could force OpenAI to unwind its current structure, require leadership changes, or reshape how AI companies balance nonprofit missions with commercial viability—setting precedent across the industry.

## The Complaint and Counterclaims

Musk alleges OpenAI strayed from its stated goal of developing AI for humanity's benefit and that he was deceived into donating as a charitable contribution to a nonprofit that later commercialized aggressively. OpenAI denies the charges, arguing Musk is retaliating after founding his own AI lab, xAI. The lawsuit was filed in 2024, though the feud dates to Musk's departure from OpenAI years earlier. Both sides have assembled heavyweight legal teams; executives are expected to testify. Early courtroom moments have already turned theatrical—Musk promoted a New Yorker profile questioning Altman's integrity, and a judge's quip about "federal government funding" appeared to needle Musk's DOGE involvement.

## Implications for AI Governance and Org Structure

The trial matters beyond OpenAI because it tests whether nonprofit structures can legally shield founders and executives from accountability once commercialization accelerates. Enterprise AI leaders and board members should monitor rulings on fiduciary duty within hybrid nonprofit-for-profit models, a structure increasingly copied across AI startups seeking both philanthropic credibility and venture capital. A verdict requiring structural unwinding could trigger similar challenges at other companies. The case also signals that AI's most contentious governance questions—alignment of stated missions with actual business models, founder accountability, and capital flows in deep-tech ventures—will likely be litigated rather than self-regulated. Organizations weighing nonprofit-to-for-profit transitions should expect increased scrutiny of founding documents and mission statements.
items.original_title: Musk v. Altman Kicks Off, DOJ Guts Voting Rights Unit, and Is the AI Job Apocalypse Overhyped?

items.headline: OpenAI Adds Hardware Security Keys to ChatGPT via Yubico Partnership
items.body:
OpenAI has launched Advanced Account Security, an opt-in protection tier for ChatGPT users that addresses a documented rise in account takeovers and phishing attacks targeting AI platform users. The company partnered with hardware security firm Yubico to integrate physical security keys into ChatGPT's authentication system, a move that reflects growing concern across the AI industry about unauthorized access to sensitive data stored in chat histories.

## The Security Key Partnership

OpenAI and Yubico co-branded two YubiKey products for the rollout: the YubiKey C NFC and YubiKey C Nano. These small hardware devices generate unique cryptographic identifiers that allow only their physical possessor to log into a connected account. Users insert the key into a USB port during login, creating a second authentication factor that traditional passwords alone cannot replace. The partnership specifically targets phishing, which exploits user credentials rather than system vulnerabilities—a threat that conventional password-based security cannot fully mitigate.

While OpenAI positioned Advanced Account Security as valuable for high-risk populations like journalists, political dissidents, and elected officials, the company emphasized availability to any user willing to adopt it. Enterprise customers storing proprietary information in ChatGPT conversations represent a natural constituency, given that compromised accounts expose potentially sensitive business data.

## The Real Cost of Stronger Protection

The tradeoff for this security improvement is substantial. OpenAI explicitly stated it cannot recover account access if a security key is lost, meaning users risk permanent loss of their conversation history. This recovery limitation distinguishes hardware-based authentication from password-reset workflows and creates a genuine operational friction point for users, particularly those relying on ChatGPT for ongoing work or research.

The timing reflects broader industry momentum around AI security. Anthropic recently unveiled a cybersecurity-focused AI model called Mythos, while OpenAI simultaneously announced a new digital defense framework. These moves signal that security is becoming a competitive differentiator in the AI platform market, driven by documented evidence that bad actors target chatbot users for extortion and data theft. For enterprise practitioners, the implication is clear: account security now merits the same deliberation applied to other infrastructure decisions, with hardware authentication emerging as the current standard for high-value deployments.
items.original_title: OpenAI announces new advanced security for ChatGPT accounts, including a partnership with Yubico

items.headline: Musk Admits xAI Used OpenAI Models to Train Grok
items.body:
Elon Musk testified in federal court Thursday that his AI startup xAI has used OpenAI's models to improve its own systems through model distillation, a technique where one AI model trains another. The admission emerged during questioning in the ongoing California lawsuit between Musk and OpenAI co-founder Sam Altman over the direction of OpenAI. When directly asked whether xAI had distilled OpenAI's technology, Musk initially deflected by noting that "generally all the AI companies" engage in the practice, then conceded it was "partly" true when pressed further.

## The Distillation Practice

Model distillation involves using a larger, more capable AI model as a "teacher" to transfer knowledge to a smaller "student" model. While the technique is standard within organizations—companies routinely distill their own models to create cheaper, faster versions for deployment—it exists in a legal and ethical gray area when applied across company boundaries. Musk characterized the practice as normal validation work, stating: "It is standard practice to use other AIs to validate your AI."

The controversy reflects broader industry tension. OpenAI and Anthropic have publicly accused Chinese competitors including DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax of illicitly distilling their models to rapidly acquire capabilities without bearing development costs. Google has similarly taken defensive measures against what it terms "distillation attacks," classifying unauthorized distillation as intellectual property theft that violates its terms of service.

## Murky Legal Territory

Musk's courtroom acknowledgment underscores how distillation occupies contested legal ground. As Anthropic noted in a recent blog post, the practice itself is legitimate when used to create internal product variations, but becomes problematic when competitors use it to acquire advanced capabilities at a fraction of independent development costs. No clear regulatory framework currently governs what constitutes permissible validation versus improper competitive extraction.

For enterprise AI practitioners and organizations evaluating their own model security posture, the case signals that distillation-based knowledge transfer between systems operated by different entities will likely face increasing scrutiny. Companies should review both their terms of service language around model usage and their defensive measures to prevent unauthorized distillation. As litigation around AI training practices expands, the distinction between standard industry practice and IP violation will increasingly depend on contractual terms and demonstrable intent—not technical capability alone.
items.original_title: Elon Musk confirms xAI used OpenAI’s models to train Grok

items.headline: Musk admits xAI used distillation on OpenAI models to train Grok
items.body:
During testimony in his lawsuit against OpenAI, Elon Musk acknowledged that xAI employed distillation techniques on OpenAI's models to develop Grok, effectively confirming what industry observers have long suspected: major AI labs routinely extract knowledge from competitors' public systems. When asked directly whether xAI used distillation on OpenAI models, Musk said the practice was "general" among AI companies and answered "partly" when pressed for a yes-or-no response. The admission surfaces a strategic vulnerability in how frontier AI labs protect their competitive moat.

## The Practice Under Fire

Distillation involves systematically querying publicly accessible chatbots and APIs to understand model behavior and capabilities, then using that knowledge to train competing systems. OpenAI and Anthropic have publicly opposed the technique, particularly when used by Chinese firms to create open-weight models that match U.S. capabilities at lower cost. However, Musk's testimony suggests American labs have applied the same approach against each other. The legality of distillation remains ambiguous—it likely violates terms of service rather than copyright law, but frontier labs have begun collaborating through the Frontier Model Forum to develop countermeasures, including preventing suspicious mass queries.

Later in his testimony, Musk ranked global AI providers differently than his public statements suggested. He placed Anthropic first, followed by OpenAI, Google, and Chinese open-source models, while characterizing xAI as a much smaller operation with a few hundred employees—a notable downgrade from his previous claim that xAI would soon surpass all competitors except Google.

## Why This Matters for Enterprise Builders

The admission undercuts a core argument frontier labs make when defending their own training practices against copyright claims: that they need massive proprietary datasets to maintain technical leadership. If leading companies can achieve competitive capability through distillation from public systems, the justification for aggressive data acquisition becomes weaker. For enterprises building on top of these models, this signals instability in the competitive positioning of major providers. A lab's claimed technological lead may rest partly on knowledge extracted from competitors rather than proprietary breakthroughs, affecting long-term reliability and differentiation. The practical take-away: evaluate AI providers on their transparent technical contributions rather than marketing claims, and assume capability parity will spread faster than vendors suggest.
items.original_title: Elon Musk testifies that xAI trained Grok on OpenAI models

items.headline: BioticsAI clears FDA after building $100K prototype—what healthcare founders should learn
items.body:
BioticsAI, a startup developing an AI copilot for ultrasound diagnostics, gained FDA approval in January after a deliberate three-year cycle of clinical validation and regulatory alignment. The company built its initial prototype for under $100,000—a rare achievement in medical devices—then won TechCrunch Startup Battlefield in 2023 before moving into hospital deployment. The path reflects a fundamentally different operating model than typical software startups: healthcare founders cannot iterate quickly or ask forgiveness later. Regulatory requirements demand upfront rigor, clinical evidence, and institutional partnership before any product reaches patients.

## Building for Regulators Before Launch

BioticsAI embedded FDA compliance into product development from day one rather than treating regulation as an afterthought. The team integrated clinical validation, regulatory strategy, and engineering into a single process, working closely with clinicians to collect large-scale datasets and run structured studies before submission. CEO Robhy Bustami emphasized that early engagement with regulators through pre-submission meetings reduced uncertainty about study design and approval expectations. This approach eliminated the common startup pattern of building first, then scrambling to meet regulatory requirements—a costly miscalculation in healthcare where redesign cycles can add years.

The company's low-cost prototype phase proved critical. Under $100,000 in initial development demonstrated feasibility without requiring Series A funding, giving the team runway to pursue clinical evidence and regulatory strategy in parallel. Once FDA clearance arrived, the business could shift immediately into hospital deployment and expansion beyond obstetrics into broader reproductive health applications.

## The Timing Problem Healthcare Creates

FDA clearance solves one risk but creates another: internal motivation over multi-year timelines. At BioticsAI, keeping engineers, clinicians, and researchers aligned required constant visibility into incremental wins—clinical study milestones, partnership announcements, early deployment successes—rather than waiting years for a final approval event. Teams need to see progress in their own scope or motivation erodes.

For AI practitioners building in regulated spaces, the lesson is structural. The startup playbook of speed and iteration doesn't transfer. Instead, regulatory approval becomes a product milestone that must be planned alongside engineering and fundraising from inception. Risk persists—investors rightly ask what happens if the FDA says no—but BioticsAI's path shows that early regulatory engagement, clinical partnerships, and deliberate study design compress uncertainty more than blind submission does. The real advantage accrues to founders who treat regulation as a design constraint, not a gate to pass through later.
items.original_title: FDA approval, fundraising, and the reality of building in healthcare according to BioticsAI founder

items.headline: Google Rolls Gemini AI Into Millions of Vehicles, Starting With GM Cars
items.body:
Google announced Thursday that it will begin deploying Gemini, its advanced conversational AI model, to vehicles equipped with Google built-in—a substantial step up from the Google Assistant currently in use. General Motors revealed separately that Gemini will reach approximately 4 million vehicles from model year 2022 onward across Cadillac, Chevrolet, Buick, and GMC. Google's broader announcement suggests the rollout extends beyond GM, though the company did not name additional automakers at this time.

## Deployment Scope and Capabilities

The rollout begins in the U.S. with English-language support, with expansion planned over coming months. Notably, Gemini won't be limited to new vehicles—existing compatible cars can receive the upgrade via software update. Drivers signed into Google accounts will be prompted to enable the feature, accessible via voice commands, on-screen microphone, or steering wheel controls.

Gemini in vehicles supports context-aware, multi-turn interactions that the existing Assistant cannot match. A driver could request a highly-rated sit-down restaurant with outdoor seating along their route, and Gemini can surface options via Google Maps, then handle follow-up queries about parking, menu options, and dietary restrictions. The system also handles conventional tasks—adjusting climate controls, providing directions, recommending music, retrieving vehicle diagnostics, and summarizing incoming messages.

Google is also testing Gemini Live, a beta feature enabling more open-ended, real-time conversations. Drivers activate it by tapping an interface button or saying "Hey Google, let's talk," unlocking brainstorming and discussion capabilities during drives.

## The Automotive AI Inflection Point

The move reflects a broader industry shift toward replacing task-specific voice assistants with generative AI models capable of understanding nuance and maintaining conversational context. For Google, the automotive vertical represents a captive, safety-sensitive user base where its core services—Maps, Search, Gmail, Calendar—can integrate seamlessly with vehicle operations.

The deployment also signals Google's intent to deepen integration with its broader service ecosystem. The company stated that future updates will extend Gemini's connection to Gmail, Google Calendar, and Google Home, creating a coherent data environment across devices and contexts.

For enterprise stakeholders, the automotive deployment represents a high-stakes testing ground for conversational AI in safety-critical, multi-modal environments. As Google expands language and regional support, the infrastructure and integration patterns established now will shape how it monetizes and evolves AI in transportation for years ahead.
items.original_title: Google’s Gemini AI assistant is hitting the road in millions of vehicles

items.headline: Musk Acknowledges xAI Used OpenAI Models for Training in Court
items.body:
During cross-examination in his ongoing legal battle with OpenAI, Elon Musk testified that xAI has used OpenAI's AI models to train its own systems. When pressed by OpenAI attorney William Savitt on whether xAI had employed distillation—a technique where one AI model trains a smaller, more efficient model—Musk initially deflected by noting that "generally all the AI companies" use this practice. He later acknowledged the practice as "partly" occurring at xAI and characterized using competitors' models to validate performance as "standard practice" in the industry.

## Model Distillation and Competitive Pressure

Distillation represents one of the fastest routes to capable AI systems: a company can reverse-engineer performance characteristics by observing how a larger model behaves, then build a smaller system that mimics that behavior at lower computational cost. Musk's testimony indicates xAI has relied on this approach using OpenAI technology. OpenAI has made clear it views this as a threat. In a February 2026 memo to Congress, the company said it had "taken steps to protect and harden our models against distillation," explicitly mentioning concerns about Chinese labs like DeepSeek appropriating American AI innovation. The Trump administration has aligned with this position, with the White House committing to share intelligence with U.S. AI companies about foreign distillation attempts.

The competitive environment has hardened considerably. Anthropic blocked OpenAI's access to its Claude coding models in August 2025 after alleging terms-of-service violations, then cut off xAI's access entirely more recently. These walls suggest industry consensus that sharing models with competitors now poses unacceptable risk.

## Strategic Implications for the Field

Musk's court admission exposes the gap between industry rhetoric around openness and actual competitive practice. While companies defend distillation as legitimate technical validation, OpenAI's legal strategy and policy lobbying reveal it as strategically unacceptable once a company reaches market dominance. For enterprise practitioners, the takeaway is clear: assume your model outputs constitute competitive intelligence once released into any external environment. If proprietary performance matters, treat model access as tightly as source code. The regulatory environment—with government now tracking foreign distillation—suggests policy will increasingly codify which distillation is acceptable and which isn't, making access controls a compliance necessity rather than a courtesy.
items.original_title: Elon Musk Seemingly Admits xAI Has Used OpenAI’s Models to Train Its Own

items.headline: OpenAI Adds High-Security Mode, Removes Support Access to Block Account Takeovers
items.body:
OpenAI launched Advanced Account Security on Thursday, an optional protection tier that eliminates passwords and support-assisted recovery in favor of hardware-based authentication. The move targets high-risk users—journalists, researchers, political dissidents, and elected officials—whose compromised accounts could expose sensitive personal and professional data. The feature reflects growing recognition that mainstream AI services now function as centralized repositories for sensitive information and connected workflows, making them attractive targets for sophisticated attackers.

## Hardware Keys and No Support Backdoors

Users who enable Advanced Account Security must replace passwords with physical security keys or passkeys, eliminating a primary attack vector for phishing. The service removes email and SMS-based account recovery entirely, forcing users to rely instead on recovery keys, backup passkeys, or physical security keys. This architectural shift deliberately cuts off a common social engineering pathway: attackers can no longer contact support teams to regain access, since support personnel lose all recovery capabilities once the feature activates.

OpenAI partnered with Yubico to provide discounted YubiKey bundles to enrolled users. The service also enforces shorter session windows and mandatory re-authentication on devices, while defaulting affected users to opt-out of conversation data being used for model training. Login alerts push users to review active sessions, creating visibility into account access patterns.

## The High-Security Tradeoff

The security gains come with friction. Users lose the convenience of password-based access and support-assisted account recovery—a meaningful usability cost for the protection achieved. However, the requirement signals which organizations now recognize the stakes. OpenAI is mandating Advanced Account Security for members of its Trusted Access for Cyber program starting June 1, unless they can attest to equivalent phishing-resistant authentication through enterprise SSO systems.

This establishes a tiered security model: mainstream users continue with standard protections, while high-value or high-risk accounts migrate to hardware-dependent systems. The move aligns with decade-old practices at Google and Microsoft but represents overdue standardization as AI services consolidate access to critical workflows and sensitive information. For enterprises integrating ChatGPT or Codex into regulated environments, the model suggests OpenAI expects account compromise to remain a material risk that users—not the platform alone—must actively manage.
items.original_title: OpenAI Rolls Out ‘Advanced’ Security Mode for At-Risk Accounts

items.headline: Stripe Link Lets AI Agents Spend Money Without Accessing Your Credentials
items.body:
Stripe has launched Link, a digital wallet that allows autonomous AI agents to make purchases on behalf of users while keeping payment credentials secure. The move addresses a practical constraint in the emerging agentic AI market: users want AI systems to handle transactions autonomously—booking reservations, buying tickets, shopping—but hesitate to grant them direct access to payment information. Link provides a permissions-based alternative, requiring user approval for individual transactions while preventing agents from ever touching raw payment data.

## How the Wallet Works

Users connect Link via standard OAuth authentication and grant their AI agent access to the wallet. When an agent needs to make a purchase, it generates a spend request with transaction details for user review. The user receives a notification, approves or denies the request, and only then does Stripe share a one-time-use virtual card or Shared Payment Token with the agent to complete the transaction. Link supports traditional payment methods—cards, bank transfers, crypto wallets, and buy-now-pay-later services—with support for stablecoins and agentic-specific payment types coming later.

The wallet itself functions as a standard digital wallet for individual users, offering spending visibility, subscription tracking, payment method management, and 90-day purchase protection. It runs on iOS, Android, and web. Developers building AI assistants or autonomous agents can also adopt Link instead of building custom wallet infrastructure.

Stripe built Link on top of its new Issuing for Agents platform, which handles real-time authorization and spending controls for agent-initiated transactions. The capability arrives as autonomous AI adoption accelerates—demand for always-on agents has reportedly become high enough that Apple sold out of base-model Mac Minis favored for running them locally.

## The Bet on Agentic Commerce

The wallet represents Stripe's calculation that significant transaction volume will flow through autonomous AI systems in the near term. Rather than compete with third-party wallet providers, Stripe is positioning Link as infrastructure for the agentic layer itself, offering developers and users a standardized way to manage agent spending permissions.

Near-term, users will need to approve individual transactions. But Stripe plans to expand controls to allow spending limits and scheduled agent autonomy—a graduated trust model that reflects realistic user comfort with delegated financial authority. For enterprises and developers, the move reduces friction: agents built on Link won't require custom payment integrations, and users won't face repeated credential-sharing friction. This could meaningfully accelerate adoption of practical agentic workflows in commerce.
items.original_title: Stripe introduces Link, a digital wallet that autonomous AI agents can use, too

items.headline: Meta's Manus unit ran undisclosed influencer scheme promoting AI money-making claims
items.body:
Meta's Manus division, acquired for $2 billion last year, ran a coordinated campaign across TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube promoting its AI tools through paid creators who failed to disclose their commercial relationships with the company. The campaign centered on a website-building scheme promising users could generate $5,000 monthly by having AI create websites for local businesses and selling them directly. Manus paid third-party agencies to deploy networks of creator accounts that obscured their ties to the company, raising questions about Meta's oversight of subsidiary operations and compliance with platform advertising rules.

## The Campaign Structure

Manus worked with external agencies to recruit and manage dozens of content creators, most in their late teens or early twenties, to produce near-identical promotional content across platforms. The accounts posted videos featuring language like "don't get a part-time job," "making thousands of dollars without talking," and "the art of Manus," with many accounts only months old and posting exclusively about Manus products. LinkedIn profiles identified at least one hire as a "viral growth expert" tasked with leading 10-20 content creators and enforcing brand guidelines, suggesting systematic rather than organic promotion.

The undisclosed creator posts coexisted with officially labeled Manus advertisements on the same platforms. When The Verge investigated, TikTok removed creator accounts and videos associated with the campaign. Manus spokesperson Ronghui Li acknowledged working with "external partners" on paid user-generated content programs but claimed responsibility for disclosure lay with creators themselves. The company declined to provide evidence supporting the earnings claims made in videos.

## Legal and Regulatory Exposure

Advertising lawyers and regulators across multiple jurisdictions consider the undisclosed relationships violations of platform policy and potentially unlawful. Meta, TikTok, and YouTube all require clear disclosure of paid promotions. British advertising regulations explicitly mandate labeling for incentivized influencer content, with vague brand-adjacent language insufficient as disclosure. Similar principles apply across the EU and US. Earnings claims represent additional legal risk under consumer protection rules against misleading advertising.

Meta declined to comment on whether it was aware of the program or whether it complied with the company's own advertising policies. The silence raises a practical question for enterprise decision-makers: subsidiary oversight mechanisms at major tech companies may be inadequate to prevent policy violations at scale. For organizations evaluating partnerships with Meta subsidiaries or building on their platforms, this incident illustrates the gap between stated compliance requirements and enforcement of those requirements on internal operations.
items.original_title: Meta is running get-rich-quick ads for its AI tools

items.headline: Salesforce accelerates AI products through weekly customer feedback loops
items.body:
Salesforce is building its AI roadmap in real time by meeting with select customers as frequently as once a week, a cadence that allows the company to release products and fixes at a pace that would be difficult to sustain through traditional planning cycles. The approach reflects a deliberate choice to let customer problems shape product direction rather than betting on predetermined timelines, particularly as AI capabilities shift rapidly and enterprise needs remain unclear.

## Weekly cycles replace quarterly planning

Salesforce maintains regular feedback relationships with 18,000 customers and works with rotating groups in structured feedback loops. The company's engineering leadership describes this as a bottom-up strategy organized around themes—agent context, observability, and deterministic controls—rather than specific product releases. Engine, a travel management platform, exemplifies this model: its operations team meets with Salesforce weekly and provides direct input on product behavior. When Engine's CEO Elia Wallen tested a voice agent booking a Chicago hotel and found the interaction felt unnatural, his feedback led to rapid changes that improved A/B test results.

Muralidhar Krishnaprasad, Salesforce's president and chief technology officer of engineering, noted the shift required structural changes. "We can't wait three months or six months to get feedback," he said. "We are literally reacting to it, week by week, month by month." This velocity matters because the company identified a critical gap: when large language models emerged, enterprises wanted to adopt them but lacked the infrastructure to deploy them effectively. Salesforce's Agentforce agent management platform emerged directly from this problem.

## Speed as competitive advantage in uncertain terrain

The model creates mutual benefit. Customers gain early access to tools before broad release and influence product direction; Salesforce gains clarity about real-world implementation challenges that pure research cannot surface. PenFed, a federal credit union, has been able to consolidate its technology stack by working closely with Salesforce on customized workflows.

For enterprise AI practitioners, this signals a fundamental shift in how large vendors operate: sustained customer collaboration is becoming infrastructure, not a marketing tactic. The take-away is direct: organizations with the engineering capacity and time to participate in vendor feedback loops gain competitive advantage through early access and product influence, while vendors that maintain these relationships can move faster than those relying on traditional product cycles. In a rapidly shifting AI landscape, this asymmetry matters.
items.original_title: Salesforce is crowdsourcing its AI roadmap — with customers

items.headline: Google Replaces Assistant with Gemini Across 4M+ Vehicles
items.body:
Google is rolling out its Gemini AI assistant to vehicles equipped with Google built-in, replacing the existing Google Assistant through software updates. The deployment begins with English-language support in the US and will expand to additional regions and languages over the coming months. The upgrade is arriving at a critical moment for in-vehicle AI, as automakers seek to move beyond command-based interfaces toward more natural conversational systems.

## Rollout Scope and Capabilities

General Motors announced yesterday that Gemini would reach 4 million vehicles from model years 2022 and newer across Cadillac, Chevrolet, Buick, and GMC brands. Google has not restricted the update to GM vehicles alone, suggesting a broader rollout across the "Google built-in" ecosystem. Users will see upgrade prompts through their Google Account login in the vehicle.

Gemini's functional improvements center on conversational flexibility. Instead of requiring memorized voice commands, drivers can now ask for restaurant recommendations, request playlist suggestions, and receive summarized text message responses. The assistant can provide real-time journey updates and answer vehicle-specific questions—a capability particularly relevant for electric vehicles, where drivers may query battery levels and charging status. Google also plans to expand access to Gmail, Calendar, and Google Home integration in the near future.

## The Broader Signal for In-Vehicle AI

This transition reflects how automakers are treating software as a competitive differentiator rather than a static feature. Google's commitment to updating existing vehicles rather than restricting new features to new purchases signals a shift toward continuous deployment in automotive contexts, where hardware lifecycles typically span 5-10 years.

For enterprise AI practitioners, the deployment raises operational questions around safety validation in safety-critical environments. In-vehicle assistants interact with drivers during active driving, making misrecognition or conversational confusion more consequential than smartphone deployments. The gradual rollout—starting with one language in one region—suggests Google is taking a cautious approach to this constraint. The broader implication: as AI systems move from phones into environments where attention is divided and stakes are higher, deployment strategies will need to account for risk differently than consumer applications have historically done.
items.original_title: Gemini is rolling out to cars with Google built-in

items.headline: Microsoft Keeps OpenAI Revenue Share as Models Go Multi-Cloud
items.body:
Microsoft and OpenAI have restructured their partnership to allow OpenAI's models and services to run on competing cloud platforms, ending the exclusive arrangement that defined their relationship since 2019. The shift, formalized this week, reflects OpenAI's $50 billion deal with Amazon and represents a significant realignment of the AI infrastructure market. Despite losing exclusivity, Microsoft secured a 20 percent revenue share from all OpenAI products across any cloud provider—a concession that transforms what initially looked like a partnership breakdown into a mutually beneficial separation.

## The Restructured Terms

OpenAI models including Codex will now ship to AWS and potentially Google Cloud within minutes of their Azure release. Microsoft retains its position as the "primary cloud partner" and continues receiving early access to new products. The revised license runs through 2032, extending two years beyond the previous 2030 endpoint. Critically, Microsoft no longer shares 20 percent of its Azure OpenAI revenue back to OpenAI, and avoids revenue-sharing obligations if its search advertising grows 15 percent year-over-year. Microsoft maintains a 27 percent stake in OpenAI's for-profit arm.

The most substantive change involves removal of the artificial general intelligence (AGI) clause that previously threatened to terminate Microsoft's access to OpenAI's most advanced models upon AGI achievement. This contentious provision had fueled over a year of conflict, including tensions when OpenAI considered acquiring AI coding tool Windsurf and explored claims of Microsoft anticompetitive behavior.

## Strategic Positioning and Enterprise Implications

For enterprises, this shift means OpenAI's latest capabilities will soon be available across cloud providers rather than concentrated on Azure. The multi-cloud approach addresses OpenAI's stated concern that enterprise customers—particularly those invested in AWS through Bedrock—faced artificial friction adopting OpenAI tools. Amazon's willingness to invest $50 billion created leverage that Microsoft couldn't counter with exclusivity arguments.

Microsoft's decision to accept revenue sharing rather than litigation suggests confidence in its alternative AI capabilities. The company has invested heavily in developing proprietary models under Mustafa Suleyman and reportedly views the arrangement as reducing long-term dependence on OpenAI. For practitioners, the practical takeaway is straightforward: lock-in through exclusive cloud partnerships is eroding. Expect multi-cloud AI strategies to become standard, with pricing and integration speed—not availability—as the actual competitive differentiators.
items.original_title: Here’s how the new Microsoft and OpenAI deal breaks down

items.headline: Goodfire's Silico Tool Brings Mechanistic Interpretability to Model Training
items.body:
Goodfire, a San Francisco startup, released Silico, a tool that lets researchers and engineers examine and adjust AI model parameters during training. The tool applies mechanistic interpretability—the practice of mapping neurons and their connections to understand how models work—to the development process itself, not just post-hoc audits. This addresses a fundamental problem: large language models achieve impressive results through processes their creators don't fully understand, making it difficult to fix flaws or prevent unwanted behaviors.

## Inside the Black Box Made Adjustable

Silico automates the complex work of tracing individual neurons and the pathways connecting them. Users can zoom in on specific neurons or groups, run experiments to see what activates them, and trace how they influence other neurons in the network. The tool applies primarily to open-source models; access to proprietary systems like ChatGPT or Gemini remains restricted.

Goodfire demonstrated the approach with concrete examples. Researchers identified a neuron in Qwen 3 associated with the trolley problem; activating it reframed model responses as explicit moral dilemmas. In another case, they found that boosting neurons linked to transparency flipped a model's answer on AI disclosure from no to yes in nine of ten trials. The company also addressed a common quirk—models incorrectly ranking 9.11 as greater than 9.9—by identifying and retraining around neurons influenced by Bible verses or code repository numbering conventions.

## Lowering Barriers to Model Development

The release democratizes techniques previously confined to major AI labs like Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google DeepMind. Smaller firms and research teams can now build or adapt open-source models with finer control over behavior. Goodfire charges on a case-by-case basis but hasn't disclosed pricing.

A key take-away for enterprises: mechanistic interpretability remains imprecise. Leonard Bereska, a researcher at the University of Amsterdam, cautioned that the approach adds "precision to the alchemy" rather than transforming model building into true engineering. Still, tools that expose parameter adjustments during training could meaningfully shift how AI development happens—moving it from pure scaling assumptions toward targeted design. For organizations building custom models or deploying adaptations of open-source systems, this represents a step toward audit-friendly, behavior-controlled AI systems.
items.original_title: This startup’s new mechanistic interpretability tool lets you debug LLMs

items.headline: X Rebuilds Ad Platform With AI to Recover Lost Revenue Ground
items.body:
X has begun rolling out a rebuilt advertising platform powered by AI-driven ranking and retrieval systems, marking a strategic push to accelerate ad revenue growth after years of decline under Elon Musk's ownership. The phased rollout targets marketers with tools for campaign creation, targeting, and optimization, positioning AI as the central mechanism for improving ad relevance and delivery. The move signals that X and its merged xAI subsidiary are treating ad platform modernization as a core priority rather than a secondary initiative.

## Revenue Recovery in Motion

X's ad business contracted sharply after Musk's 2022 acquisition, forcing the company to diversify into subscriptions and AI services. However, eMarketer forecasts show a turnaround underway: projected ad revenue of $2.26 billion in 2025 rising to $2.46 billion in 2026. While still representing roughly half of Twitter's 2021 peak, the trajectory reflects stabilization and incremental growth. The rebuilt platform aims to accelerate that recovery by enabling advertisers to create and control campaigns more efficiently while using AI to optimize performance automatically.

Monique Pintarelli, head of global advertising at xAI, framed the rebuild as representative of the combined entity's capabilities: building complex systems rapidly with technical depth. The new stack is designed to support continuous feature releases and faster integration of emerging tools, suggesting X intends this platform as a foundation for iterative improvement rather than a one-time overhaul.

## Where AI-Driven Adtech Creates Real Leverage

The broader tech industry has demonstrated that AI-powered advertising drives measurable results. Google, Meta, and others reported strong earnings this week, with AI automating critical ad functions—creation, targeting, measurement—across their platforms. This automation has lowered barriers for smaller advertisers, democratizing access to enterprise-grade tools. X's rebuild follows this proven pattern, betting that better AI infrastructure will attract spending from advertisers already seeing returns on AI-assisted campaigns elsewhere.

For enterprises evaluating X as an advertising channel, the key question is execution speed and competitive parity. X operates at a disadvantage: Meta and Google have years of AI optimization embedded in their ad systems, larger data sets, and deeper advertiser relationships. A rebuilt platform signals intent but not certainty. The concrete signal to watch is whether the phased rollout delivers measurable improvements in campaign performance and advertiser retention, not platform announcements.
items.original_title: X announces a rebuilt ad platform powered by AI

items.headline: OpenAI's Goblin Problem: How a Training Quirk Spread Across Models
items.body:
OpenAI has published an explanation for why its AI models developed an unusual habit of referencing goblins, gremlins, and other mythological creatures in their outputs. The issue emerged as a side effect of reinforcement learning during model training, where a specific reward structure inadvertently incentivized the quirky language pattern. The company's disclosure reveals how subtle training dynamics can produce unexpected behavioral artifacts that persist across model generations and prove difficult to fully eliminate.

## How the Quirk Spread

The goblin references first appeared with GPT-5.1's "Nerdy" personality option, where OpenAI's reinforcement training rewarded the metaphorical language as part of that distinct voice. The behavior worsened with subsequent releases as newer models trained on data containing these previously rewarded outputs. OpenAI notes that while the rewards were initially scoped to the Nerdy condition, reinforcement learning doesn't guarantee learned behaviors remain isolated. Once a pattern gets rewarded, later training stages can spread it elsewhere, particularly when those outputs are reused in supervised fine-tuning or preference data.

When OpenAI discontinued the Nerdy personality in March, goblin references dropped significantly but didn't vanish entirely. GPT-5.5's Codex coding tool still exhibited the behavior because model training had begun before the root cause was identified. Rather than retraining from scratch, OpenAI implemented direct instructions prohibiting the models from discussing the creatures—a pragmatic intervention rather than a systematic fix. The company has also shared instructions allowing users to reverse these restrictions if preferred.

## What This Reveals About Model Training

The incident illustrates a fundamental challenge in modern AI training: emergent behaviors that arise from optimization processes can spread unpredictably across different model versions and use cases. For enterprises deploying large language models, this underscores the importance of monitoring model outputs for unexpected patterns, particularly when models are fine-tuned or when training data incorporates outputs from previous model versions.

The goblin problem itself is harmless, but the mechanism behind it—reward functions propagating unintended behaviors across model generations—has direct implications for safety and consistency in production systems. Organizations should expect that training choices made for specific use cases may have downstream effects on broader model behavior. The takeaway: AI systems require iterative monitoring and refinement, and quick patches may be necessary while more fundamental retraining occurs.
items.original_title: OpenAI talks about not talking about goblins

items.headline: Spotify Launches Verification Badge to Distinguish Human Artists From AI
items.body:
Spotify is rolling out a verification program designed to help listeners identify authentic human artists amid rising volumes of AI-generated music. The "Verified by Spotify" badge and green checkmark will appear on artist profiles after the company confirms a real person operates the account and uploads the music. The initiative addresses a concrete problem: AI music submissions have proliferated across streaming platforms, with competitor Deezer reporting that AI uploads nearly match human-created music on its service. Spotify says more than 99% of artists that listeners actively search for will receive verification at launch, including a significant portion of independent musicians alongside major-label acts.

## Verification Criteria and Scope

Spotify is not relying on traditional identity documents. Instead, the company cross-references activity across multiple channels: streaming engagement, social media presence, merchandise sales, and tour dates. Artists must demonstrate "consistent listener activity and engagement over time," effectively creating a threshold that excludes low-traffic profiles. The company notes that artists below royalty-payment thresholds may not qualify for verification. At launch, AI personas and profiles primarily featuring AI-generated music are explicitly ineligible. However, Spotify has signaled openness to verifying AI acts in the future, acknowledging that "the concept of artist authenticity is complex and quickly evolving."

## The Broader Signal on AI Music Classification

The verification badge reflects streaming platforms' attempt to segment AI content without outright banning it. Spotify is running parallel initiatives—it already offers voluntary AI labels for songs and is beta-testing "artist detail" labels similar to nutritional information panels. This layered approach suggests platforms are moving toward transparency and curation rather than prohibition. For enterprises building music-related AI tools or content management systems, the signal is clear: platforms will increasingly demand disclosure and verifiable metadata. The openness to future AI verification also indicates that the industry has not settled on whether AI music should be treated as a separate category or integrated into standard discovery and credibility systems. Organizations planning to distribute AI-generated music should expect authentication requirements to tighten and expect that verification pathways—and their criteria—will evolve as listener expectations solidify.
items.original_title: Verified by Spotify badge lets you know this artist isn’t AI

items.headline: Research outlines reliability standards for AI systems deployed in clinical settings
items.body:
A research initiative published in July 2023 addresses a critical gap in healthcare AI deployment: establishing frameworks for building reliable AI tools that can operate alongside clinicians in real-world medical environments. The work tackles the tension between AI's demonstrated capability in narrow tasks and the stringent safety requirements of clinical practice, where errors directly affect patient outcomes.

## Building Trust Through Systematic Reliability

The research focuses on developing methodologies to evaluate and ensure AI system dependability across the clinical workflow. Rather than treating AI as a standalone diagnostic tool, the framework positions these systems as co-clinicians—tools that integrate into existing decision-making processes and require measurable performance guarantees across diverse patient populations and edge cases.

Key concerns addressed include consistency across different demographic groups, robustness when encountering data patterns not present in training sets, and transparent communication of uncertainty. The work recognizes that clinical adoption depends not on achieving superhuman performance in isolated benchmarks, but on demonstrating reliability under conditions that mirror actual hospital operations, where data quality varies, urgent decisions cannot wait for perfect information, and clinicians need clear signals about when to trust or question an AI recommendation.

## Implications for Enterprise Deployment

For organizations implementing healthcare AI, this research signals a shift from proof-of-concept validation toward production-grade reliability standards. The framework addresses a persistent deployment bottleneck: healthcare institutions require evidence of consistent performance before integrating AI into clinical workflows, yet vendors often lack standardized ways to demonstrate this.

The practical takeaway is that successful healthcare AI requires investing upstream in reliability engineering—including adversarial testing, longitudinal validation studies, and failure mode analysis—rather than rushing systems into clinical environments. Enterprises deploying or procuring AI tools for healthcare should evaluate whether vendors can articulate their reliability standards using clinically relevant metrics rather than academic benchmarks. This shift toward reliability-first thinking will likely become a prerequisite for regulatory approval and institutional adoption.
items.original_title: Enabling a new model for healthcare with AI co-clinician

items.headline: Meta's Business AI Hits 10M Weekly Conversations, Signals Shift Toward Monetization
items.body:
Meta's business AI assistant has grown tenfold in three months, reaching 10 million conversations per week as of late March, up from 1 million at the start of 2025. The company disclosed the metric during its first-quarter earnings call Wednesday, signaling that despite limited visibility in mainstream AI product discussions, Meta's enterprise tools are capturing significant adoption among small businesses. The expansion follows a beta program rollout across the U.S., EMEA, APAC, and LATAM regions.

## Scaling Free Tools Before Charging

Meta is currently offering its business AI capabilities for free to small businesses across its messaging apps—WhatsApp, Instagram, and Messenger—a deliberate strategy to build scale before establishing pricing models. CEO Mark Zuckerberg acknowledged this transition point during the earnings call, stating: "Business AIs today are currently free for most businesses on our messaging apps, but as we make more progress, we expect that we will also work towards establishing a longer-term monetization model."

The business AI tools are being powered by Meta's new large language model, Muse Spark, released under the Meta Superintelligence Labs division created last year. Beyond conversation tools, Meta is also seeing strong traction with creative AI features. The company reported that more than 8 million advertisers are using at least one of its generative AI ad creative tools, with particularly strong adoption among small and medium-sized businesses. Notably, advertisers using the video generation feature have seen more than 3% higher conversion rates in tests. This week, Meta is launching the open beta of Meta Ads AI Connectors, which will let advertisers link their ad accounts to AI agents.

## Enterprise AI's Revenue Inflection Point

The timing of monetization hints reveals Meta's confidence that enterprise AI has moved from novelty to necessity. The company reported $56.3 billion in total revenue for Q1, up 33% year-over-year, with $885 million specifically from apps revenue driven by WhatsApp paid messaging and subscriptions. This baseline is relevant because business AI currently generates zero revenue—a gap Meta expects to close within the next one to two years as tool adoption stabilizes.

For enterprise buyers, the implication is clear: free tools will not remain free indefinitely. Organizations currently adopting Meta's business AI should expect pricing announcements within the next 12-18 months, likely tiered by conversation volume or feature access. Early adopters have a window to lock in workflows before pricing structures are finalized.
items.original_title: Meta says its business AI now facilitates 10 million conversations a week

items.headline: Meta loses 20M users while doubling down on $10B AI spending surge
items.body:
Meta reported a 20 million user decline in its latest quarter while simultaneously announcing a $10 billion increase in annual capital expenditure, bringing total projected spending to $125-145 billion for 2026. The divergent moves signal the company's aggressive pivot toward AI infrastructure even as its core social platforms face headwinds. The spending jump reflects what Meta's chief financial officer Susan Li characterized as underestimation of compute demand, positioning the company for an expensive race to secure AI capabilities.

## The Numbers Behind the Retreat

Meta's "Family daily active people"—the company's aggregate measure across Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger—fell by 20 million users compared to the previous quarter. The company attributed the decline to internet disruptions in Iran and restrictions on WhatsApp access in Russia. However, this bundled reporting obscures which individual platforms were most affected, making independent assessment difficult.

Revenue growth painted a different picture. Meta's quarterly revenue surged 33 percent year-over-year to $56.3 billion, the fastest growth rate since 2021. Yet Reality Labs, Meta's VR and wearables division, posted an operating loss of $4.03 billion for the quarter, following two rounds of layoffs since January. These losses underscored the financial drag from hardware bets that have failed to gain traction.

## Compute Demand and Strategic Priorities

Meta's capital expenditure increase targets higher component pricing and additional data center capacity, with Li's acknowledgment of past underestimation suggesting the company views AI infrastructure as non-negotiable. The $10 billion revision signals confidence in AI's competitive importance, even as near-term user metrics deteriorate and Reality Labs hemorrhages capital.

For enterprise practitioners, Meta's spending trajectory matters because it reflects broader infrastructure competition among cloud and AI leaders. The company's willingness to absorb Reality Labs losses while accelerating AI investment indicates where leadership sees future margins and differentiation. The user decline, while attributed to geopolitical factors, warrants monitoring—sustained losses in family DAU could constrain advertising inventory and pressure the revenue growth that funds these investments. Meta's bet is that AI capabilities will ultimately drive engagement and monetization gains large enough to justify current spending. Whether that calculus holds will become clearer as compute deployments scale through 2026 and 2027.
items.original_title: Meta lost 20 million users last quarter

items.headline: OpenAI restricts cybersecurity model to vetted defenders
items.body:
OpenAI is launching GPT-5.5-Cyber, a specialized AI model built for cybersecurity work, but will not release it to the public. CEO Sam Altman announced the limited rollout will begin within days, distributed only to vetted "cyber defenders" and institutions. The move reflects a widening industry pattern of gating access to frontier AI models deemed too risky for unrestricted use, even as competition intensifies to deploy advanced capabilities.

## Controlled Rollout and Unclear Access Criteria

OpenAI has not disclosed who qualifies as a trusted cyber defender or published technical specifications for the model. Altman said the company will work with "the entire ecosystem and the government" to determine access rules. Previous OpenAI "trusted access" programs have involved vetted professionals and institutions, but specifics remain opaque. GPT-5.5-Cyber is a specialized version of GPT-5.5, OpenAI's recently released flagship model. The company has staggered releases of earlier cybersecurity models and its life sciences model, GPT-Rosalind, which targets drug discovery and biology research.

The approach mirrors Anthropic's rollout of Claude Mythos this month, though Anthropic's execution faced public complications around secure distribution. White House officials have already taken interest in restricting Mythos access further, citing both cybersecurity risks from wider distribution and concern that demand surge would constrain government use of the system.

## Industry Gatekeeping and Policy Tensions

The staggered-release strategy now functions as a competitive differentiator: vendors brand restricted models as too dangerous for public deployment, implicitly positioning them as more capable than freely available alternatives. This framing justifies premium pricing and government partnerships while sidestepping regulatory scrutiny that might otherwise apply to powerful dual-use tools.

The White House's involvement in model access decisions signals that AI governance is shifting toward direct government-vendor coordination on deployment, outside formal regulatory channels. This creates ambiguity for enterprises: access to frontier cybersecurity models may depend less on procurement capability than on government approval, and policy can shift based on shifting risk assessments. Organizations dependent on cutting-edge defense capabilities should monitor how access criteria evolve and whether alternative models from other vendors will be gated similarly. The precedent set by both OpenAI and Anthropic suggests restricted distribution will become standard for high-capability models, making vendor relationships and government standing material factors in AI strategy.
items.original_title: OpenAI’s new security model is for ‘critical cyber defenders’ only

items.headline: Google's Gemini Privacy Controls Hidden Behind Dark Patterns
items.body:
Google has integrated Gemini into Gmail, Drive, and other Workspace products while obscuring the mechanisms users need to prevent their data from feeding AI training. The company clarifies that it doesn't directly scan inboxes to train foundational models, but Gemini outputs—which can include email summaries and file snippets—become training material. Users who want to opt out face a choice between losing chat history or allowing their interactions to be used for model improvement. The privacy controls exist but are deliberately difficult to locate, exemplifying what design researchers call dark patterns.

## Gemini's Data Pipeline and Training

Google states that Workspace content remains private and is not used to train foundational AI models. However, when users interact with Gemini features in Gmail or Drive, the AI can access those files to complete specific tasks. The company attempts to "filter and reduce" personal information in training datasets derived from Gemini outputs, but provides no transparency into how effective this filtering is. To fully prevent data from entering training pipelines, users must navigate to an obscure settings page labeled only "Activity," toggle off "Gemini Apps Activity," and accept the loss of their chat history. This setting does not appear in Google's main privacy control panel where users would naturally expect to find it—a discrepancy Google acknowledged but has not corrected across multiple tested accounts.

## The Autonomy Problem for Enterprise Users

For organizations deploying Google Workspace at scale, this arrangement creates compliance and governance risks. Marie Potel of Fair Patterns notes that dark patterns—UI designs that undermine user agency regardless of intent—violate user autonomy when the only path to opting out requires accepting degraded functionality. The buried toggle forces a false binary: retain usable Gemini features with data flowing to training, or disable features entirely. Enterprise IT teams cannot easily implement consistent data governance policies when privacy controls are hidden and choosing privacy means discarding feature utility. The absence of straightforward opt-out mechanisms in account-level privacy settings signals that Google prioritizes adoption and model improvement over user choice. Organizations relying on Google Workspace need explicit vendor commitments that privacy controls will be discoverable, that opting out does not degrade core functionality, and that training on user interactions can be disabled without losing service logs.
items.original_title: The hidden cost of Google's AI defaults and the illusion of choice

items.headline: Hoffman: Doctors Not Using AI for Second Opinions Risk Malpractice
items.body:
Reid Hoffman, the LinkedIn cofounder and OpenAI board member, has made a provocative claim about the future of medical practice: physicians who don't consult frontier AI models for clinical decisions are approaching malpractice. Speaking at WIRED Health in London, Hoffman argued that large language models—even those not specifically trained on medical data—have absorbed enough information to serve as a valuable diagnostic check. The statement signals how aggressively some technologists are pushing AI into clinical workflows, even as studies document real risks from these systems in medical contexts.

## Hoffman's Drug Discovery Play

Hoffman's push into healthcare centers on Manas AI, a startup he cofounded with renowned cancer physician Siddhartha Mukherjee. The company aims to compress drug discovery timelines from a decade to a few years by using AI to identify novel cancer drug targets. Hoffman positions AI as a tool to unlock treatments for rare diseases that pharmaceutical companies have historically avoided due to limited profit margins. He projects that within ten years, AI discovery engines will identify target molecules for every major disease capable of making "a serious difference."

At Manas, human judgment remains central to the process—Mukherjee personally reviews the AI engine's proposals and filters out what Hoffman calls "bonks stupid" candidates. This hybrid approach acknowledges that AI generates raw material requiring expert curation rather than autonomous decision-making.

## The Broader Clinical Claim

Hoffman's argument extends beyond drug discovery to real-time clinical practice. He claims to personally use frontier models for his own health decisions and insists his concierge doctors do the same. His framing positions AI as a second opinion that prevents misdiagnosis—a check on human cognitive bias—rather than a replacement for physician judgment.

This stance conflicts with recent research. A major 2024 study found that large language models provide inaccurate and inconsistent medical information to the general public. The tension between Hoffman's confidence in frontier models and documented evidence of their unreliability creates a genuine gap. His proposal also carries an implicit conflict of interest: as someone building AI systems for healthcare, he benefits from regulatory and clinical adoption.

For enterprises developing clinical AI tools, the core takeaway is clearer than Hoffman's rhetoric: positioning AI as a "second opinion" that augments rather than replaces physician judgment may be more defensible both legally and clinically than claims of autonomous capability. But substantial validation work—not venture-backed optimism—will determine whether that framing holds up in practice.
items.original_title: Reid Hoffman Thinks Doctors Should Ask AI for a Second Opinion

items.headline: SoftBank launches data center robotics startup, targets $100B IPO by year-end
items.body:
SoftBank is establishing Roze AI, a new robotics company aimed at automating data center construction in the United States. The conglomerate plans to deploy autonomous robots to streamline server farm building and reduce construction inefficiencies. The venture is already being prepared for a public offering, with some executives targeting a second-half 2026 IPO at a $100 billion valuation—an aggressive timeline that has sparked internal skepticism about both the valuation and feasibility.

## The Startup Structure

Roze AI will operate as a distinct business unit focused on deploying automation technology to accelerate physical infrastructure buildout. The company would address a specific bottleneck: data center construction has become a critical constraint as AI companies scale compute capacity. By introducing robotics and autonomous systems into the building process, Roze aims to reduce timelines and labor costs—two factors limiting how quickly new facilities can come online.

The IPO ambition is notable given the venture's early stage. Planning a public listing within months of formation suggests SoftBank views the market opportunity as substantial and time-sensitive. However, internal dissent at SoftBank itself indicates skepticism about whether the business can justify a nine-figure valuation this quickly or execute an IPO at that pace.

## Automation Race and Track Record

SoftBank's move reflects a broader industry shift toward applying AI and robotics to industrial modernization. Amazon's Jeff Bezos recently co-founded Project Prometheus, which plans to acquire companies across major industrial sectors and upgrade them with AI systems. The pattern signals venture capital confidence in automation-driven infrastructure plays.

SoftBank's history with moonshot ventures is mixed. The firm invested hundreds of millions in Zume, an AI-driven pizza delivery startup that collapsed in 2023, illustrating the risks of aggressive valuations on unproven automation concepts. Roze's rapid path to IPO, coupled with internal doubt about its valuation, echoes earlier missteps. For enterprise decision-makers evaluating data center expansion, the key takeaway is that competitive pressure to automate construction is accelerating—but the viability and timeline for mature robotics solutions in this space remain unproven. Monitor Roze's actual construction outcomes and customer commitments rather than IPO announcements as indicators of real progress.
items.original_title: SoftBank is creating a robotics company that builds data centers — and already eyeing a $100B IPO

items.headline: Inference Becomes the Bottleneck as AI Shifts from Training to Deployment
items.body:
A convergence of statements from AI industry leaders signals a fundamental shift in computing infrastructure priorities. OpenAI's Sam Altman, Anthropic's Noam Brown, and NVIDIA's Jensen Huang have all recently emphasized that inference—not training—now represents the strategic constraint limiting AI capability deployment. This marks the transition from a training-dominated era to one where the ability to run models at scale in production determines competitive advantage.

## Compute Demand Eclipses Prior Investment

The numbers substantiate the inflection. NVIDIA's Huang stated that inference compute demand has increased roughly one millionfold over the past two years—a combination of a 10,000x increase in token-generation demand paired with a 100x growth in user adoption. This demand surge has created a secondary effect: Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan pointed to rising CPU utilization for inference workloads, interrupting a two-year period where enterprise spending was redirected almost entirely toward GPU acquisition. The CPU refresh cycle that would have normally occurred around 2024-2025 was deferred as organizations exhausted budgets on AI infrastructure, but now CPU capacity constraints are emerging. Reinforcement learning simulations, agentic workflows, and code execution pipelines all require CPU cycles alongside GPU inference, creating a compound resource shortage.

The market is restructuring around this bottleneck. NVIDIA acquired Groq, Intel invested in SambaNova, and Amazon moved closer to Cerebras partnerships—all companies that optimize for inference workload patterns, specifically prefill and decode disaggregation, which separates the prompt-processing phase from token-generation phases to maximize throughput. This architectural shift is now industry standard, indicating the problem is not temporary.

## Infrastructure Recalibration Required

For enterprises, the implication is direct: inference infrastructure decisions now rival model selection in strategic importance. The constraint is no longer "which model performs best" but "can we run it at the scale and latency our users demand." OpenAI's Codex platform illustrates this at the product level—recent updates optimized agent-loop systems engineering rather than model inference latency, with WebSocket-mode implementations yielding 40% faster workflows by maintaining state across tool calls. This signals that practitioners must shift optimization focus from raw model performance to system-level efficiency.

The takeaway for enterprise teams: audit your inference infrastructure assumptions immediately. If your current setup relies on GPU capacity alone or assumes CPU refresh cycles will occur normally, both assumptions are now invalid. Prefill-decode disaggregation and heterogeneous compute planning are no longer optional—they define the difference between deploying at scale and hitting ceiling. The inference inflection is already here; the question is whether your infrastructure is.
items.original_title: [AINews] The Inference Inflection

items.headline: AWS Growth Hits 15-Quarter High as Amazon Pours $59B Into AI Infrastructure
items.body:
Amazon Web Services grew 28% year-over-year in the first quarter to reach $37.6 billion in net sales—the fastest growth rate for the unit in 15 quarters.
